subject: [PATCH] mm/slub: serve slabobj_ext array from a strictly larger kmalloc cache

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Harry Yoo noted that the patch description does not clearly explain why the current solution in commit 280ea9c3154b is insufficient to prevent the stack overflow, and requested a more detailed explanation of the fix.

The reviewer noted that the patch's assumption about KMALLOC_NORMAL caches not needing obj_exts arrays may be incorrect and suggested considering a scenario where memory allocation profiling is enabled.

Reviewer Harry Yoo noted that the proposed fix will waste memory due to a larger obj_exts array size, and suggested creating a new kmalloc type to avoid cycles instead.

Reviewer Harry Yoo pointed out that the patch's logic is no longer relevant due to a change in how object sizes are compared, and suggested that the comparison should be done based on cache pointers instead of object size.
sentiment_signals: comparison should be based on cache pointers
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Shakeel,

On 6/26/26 8:00 AM, Shakeel Butt wrote:

---

Usually KMALLOC_NORMAL caches don't need obj_exts array, but yes,
this could happen if memory allocation profiling is enabled.

---

This will fix the problem.

But this will waste memory as we need smaller obj_exts array
as the size gets larger.

We should probably create a new kmalloc type to avoid cycles instead?
(needed only when memory profiling is enabled, though)

That would also prevent recursion even further.

---

This comment is no longer relevant, by the way.

"compare object_size instead of cache pointers because there can be
 multiple caches of the same size" doesn't apply anymore.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledges that the patch is intended to address a specific issue related to slabobj_ext array allocation and confirms that the fix will be implemented in a future version (v2) by restructuring the code.

Author acknowledges reviewer's concern about adding kmem caches for users not using memory profiling, but pushes back on the idea of it being a separate discussion.

The author acknowledges that the patch needs to address the issue of slabobj_ext array being served from a cache smaller than the one it describes, and agrees to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged a minor issue with a comment and promised to remove it in the next version, indicating no further revision is needed.
reviews.raw_body:
Yes, we have enabled memory allocation profiling fleet wide.

[...]

---

Yes but I assume that would add kmem caches even for users not using memory
profiling. Anyways, I think that is a separate discussion. Am I understanding
correctly that you don't have any concerns with this approach?

---

Ack.

[...]

---

I will remove the comment in next version.

Thanks for the review.

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil (SUSE) expressed concern about the memory waste caused by the patch, suggesting that the size bumping should only occur when allocation profiling is enabled and not booted with 'never'. They also questioned whether memcg-only obj_exts arrays have this issue and suggested seeking input from Suren.
reviews.raw_body:
Umm, the memory waste is a concern?

Minimally I'd now want to only do that size bumping when allocation
profiling is enabled. Ideally that means both configured in and not booted
with "never".

We probably should have done that already in 280ea9c3154b2. Because AFAIU
memcg-only obj_exts array don't have this issue (or maybe they do have the
[1] issue? Harry?). But if memcg-only should keep avoiding the same size
bucket, it can keep what it was doing and only memalloc profiling would do
the strictly larger thing.

Suren's input would be also nice to have.

Thanks!

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/oe-lkp/202601231457.f7b31e09-lkp@intel.com

2026-06-26.patch_summary: This patch fixes a kernel stack overflow issue caused by a recursive cycle between kmalloc-512 and kmalloc-1k slabs when freeing resources. The problem occurs because the obj_exts array, used for allocation profiling and memcg accounting, is allocated from the same cache as the slab it describes. To fix this, the patch changes the allocation to use a strictly larger cache than the one described by the slab, breaking the cycle and preventing the stack overflow.

2026-06-25.patch_summary: This patch fixes a kernel stack overflow issue caused by a recursive cycle between kmalloc-512 and kmalloc-1k slabs when freeing resources. The problem occurs because the obj_exts array, used for allocation profiling and memcg accounting, is allocated from the same cache as the slab it describes. To fix this, the patch changes the allocation to use a strictly larger cache than the one described by the slab, breaking the cycle and preventing the stack overflow.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author addressed a concern about the patch's impact on slabobj_ext array allocation, explaining that the relocation of the array to a larger kmalloc cache will not increase memory usage and will prevent cycles in the slab object graph.

Author acknowledged that the issue is specific to slab allocation and not memcg, and plans to explore either dedicated caches or a specialized size bumping approach, prioritizing easy backportability.
reviews.raw_body:
On Fri, Jun 26, 2026 at 07:11:33PM +0200, Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) wrote:
[...]

---

memcg should not have this issue as normal kmalloc caches do not serve memcg
charged objects. 

So here we can do dedicated caches as Harry suggested or make this size bumping
very specialized as Vlastimil suggested. What do we want long term? Orthogonally
we do want this fix to be backported easily to older stable kernels. I will see
how does this narrowed down size bumping looks like.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch's logic for handling slabobj_ext array allocation when memcg accounting is enabled or disabled, proposing an additional check to ensure that the obj_exts cache object size is strictly larger than the current slab's object size.

2026-06-27.patch_summary: This patch fixes a kernel stack overflow issue caused by a recursive cycle between kmalloc-512 and kmalloc-1k slabs when freeing resources. The problem occurs because the obj_exts array, used for allocation profiling and memcg accounting, is allocated from the same cache as the slab it describes. To fix this, the patch changes the allocation to use a strictly larger cache than the one described by the slab, breaking the cycle and preventing the stack overflow.
